# Question Tree: Proposal Approach

Stress-test the proposal strategy, win theme integration, and discriminator
credibility before the writing phase begins.

---

### 1. Compliance foundation

"Do we have a fully annotated compliance matrix? Is every 'shall' from
Section L mapped to a specific proposal section with an owner?"

_Recommended answer_: A compliance matrix is not optional. If it doesn't
exist yet, building it is the first action — before writing a single word.

---

### 2. Win theme / eval factor traceability

"For each win theme, which specific evaluation factor does it address? If a
win theme doesn't map to a scored factor, why are we leading with it?"

_Recommended answer_: Every win theme should be traceable to at least one
evaluation factor. Themes that don't map to scored areas are wasted pages.

---

### 3. Discriminators vs. qualifiers

"Pick your strongest discriminator. Now be honest: can any of the top three
competitors truthfully claim the same thing?"

_Recommended answer_: If competitors can claim the same thing, it's a
qualifier (necessary to compete) not a discriminator (reason to win). Be
brutal. Most 'discriminators' are actually qualifiers.

---

### 4. Hot button coverage

"Walk through the three hot buttons from capture. Is each one addressed in
the executive summary, and does each major section reinforce at least one?"

_Recommended answer_: Hot buttons should appear in the executive summary AND
in the body sections most relevant to them. A hot button that only shows up
once isn't integrated — it's mentioned.

---

### 5. FAB chains

"Take your most important capability claim. Can you complete: 'Our [Feature]
means [Advantage], which means you [Benefit]'?"

_Recommended answer_: Most proposals stop at Feature. The ones that score
well push through Advantage and Benefit. If you can't complete the chain,
the claim isn't ready to be written.

---

### 6. Proof points per discriminator

"State the proof point for your top discriminator. Is it specific — contract
number, metric, date, customer name — or is it generic?"

_Recommended answer_: Source selection evaluators are trained to discount
generic claims. Specific proof points, especially ones referencing work the
same customer has seen, are what move scores.

---

### 7. Ghost language exploitation

"Which exact phrases from the RFP are we playing back in our proposal? Does
our executive summary mirror the customer's own language back at them?"

_Recommended answer_: Evaluators score higher when they see their own
language reflected. Identify every phrase in the RFP that we should echo
verbatim in our approach.

---

### 8. Executive summary test

"Read the first paragraph of the executive summary aloud. Does it open with
the customer's problem and our discriminating solution — or with your company
name and founding year?"

_Recommended answer_: The executive summary should open with the customer's
problem. Company bio belongs at the end, if at all.

---

### 9. Pink team vulnerabilities

"If a Pink Team reviewed this proposal today, what's the first weakness
they'd flag? What's the evaluator concern we're least prepared to address?"

_Recommended answer_: Self-identify the weakness before a review team does.
Then either fix it or write explicitly around it.

---

### 10. Page and volume allocation

"Are the highest-weighted evaluation factors getting the most page depth?
Where are we potentially over-writing lower-weighted areas?"

_Recommended answer_: Page allocation should be proportional to evaluation
factor weights. A 20%-weighted factor shouldn't get 5% of the pages.
